slides.title: What is a Multilayer Perceptron?
slides.content: A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is a class of feedforward artificial neural network. It consists of at least three layers of nodes: an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer. MLPs are used to approximate complex functions and are a foundational model in deep learning.

slides.title: Structure of MLP
slides.content: MLPs have an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Each node in one layer connects with a certain weight to every node in the next layer.

slides.title: Activation Functions
slides.content: Activation functions introduce non-linear properties to the network. Commonly used activation functions include Sigmoid, ReLU, and Tanh. These functions help the network learn complex patterns.

slides.title: Training an MLP
slides.content: Training an MLP involves adjusting the weights based on the error of the output compared to the expected result. This is often done using a method called backpropagation.

visual_assets.code:
def train_mlp(X, y):
    model = MLPClassifier()
    model.fit(X, y)
    return model

slides.title: Applications of MLP
slides.content: MLPs are used in various applications such as image and speech recognition, and natural language processing. Their ability to model complex relationships makes them suitable for these tasks.

slides.title: Performance Evaluation
slides.content: Evaluating the performance of an MLP involves checking accuracy, precision, and recall metrics. These metrics help understand how well the model is performing on unseen data.

slides.title: Summary
slides.content: MLPs are a fundamental building block in neural networks and deep learning. Understanding their structure, training, and applications can significantly enhance machine learning projects.
